our_argument_summary: Our review says the dome model conflates regional SAA field decay (20–35 nT/yr in the SAA core) with global dipole moment decay (~15 nT/yr). The 28 nT threshold is met only by cherry-picking the SAA region, not the global average. Verdict: 'Misleading' — conflation of regional and global measurements.

relevant_files: monitor.py — Domain 8: 'SAA Decay Rate (TTB)' monitors Tsumeb station for >=28 nT/yr decay (per WIN-004 and WIN-005 curmudgeon reviews)
inject_ai_layer.py — W011 mapping to WIN-037 as static confirmed entry
raw-text/02-wins.txt — PROS-007 registered 2026-03-06, confirmed by citing 'SAA region alone dropped ~30 nT'
raw-text/05-model.txt — 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year' listed as core model parameter
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-037 maps to prediction W011 (PROS-007). Based on WIN-004 and WIN-005 curmudgeon reviews, monitor.py's Domain 8 ('SAA Decay Rate TTB') monitors the Tsumeb (TTB) INTERMAGNET station for >=28 nT/yr decay. This is a single-station observation in the SAA core region, not a global field measurement. The domain compares observed station decay rate against the hardcoded 28 nT/yr threshold. WIN-037's confirmation text explicitly says 'SAA region alone dropped ~30 nT since January 2025' — acknowledging the measurement is regional, not global, even though PROS-007 was framed as a 'global geomagnetic field' prediction.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded threshold comparison. Domain 8 checks whether TTB station decay exceeds 28 nT/yr, but the threshold itself is a static parameter. The inject_ai_layer.py file stores WIN-037 as a static confirmed entry (W011). No code dynamically computes a global field average or global dipole moment decay rate.
data_sources_used: INTERMAGNET Tsumeb (TTB) station (live data via monitor.py Domain 8)
NOAA WMM2025 (claimed confirmation source in raw text)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome prediction says 'global geomagnetic field' but confirms it with 'SAA region alone.' This built-in ambiguity allows the dome to claim credit regardless: if the global average exceeds 28 nT/yr, the prediction is confirmed globally; if only the SAA exceeds it, the prediction is confirmed regionally. The threshold cannot fail as long as any geographic region shows >=28 nT/yr decay.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not explicitly reference monitor.py Domain 8 or the Tsumeb station monitoring. This is a minor gap — our argument focuses correctly on the regional-vs-global conflation, but documenting that the dome's own monitoring infrastructure only checks a single SAA station would strengthen the argument. Note: monitor.py and pull_data.py source files are not present in the repository as extracted files — code analysis is based on cross-references from WIN-004, WIN-005, and WIN-035 curmudgeon reviews documenting Domain 8.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py Domain 8 ('SAA Decay Rate TTB') monitors Tsumeb station decay against a hardcoded >=28 nT/yr threshold. While it fetches live INTERMAGNET data for TTB, the test is a simple threshold comparison (obs >= 28) with the threshold baked in. The 'global field' framing of WIN-037 is not tested — only a single SAA station is checked. WIN-037's W011 entry in inject_ai_layer.py is stored as static confirmed metadata. Tagging as 'hardcoded' because the validation reduces to comparing a live station reading against a static threshold, with no global field computation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Geomagnetic field decay has been continuously monitored by NOAA/NCEI (WMM series), ESA/Swarm, IAGA (IGRF), and INTERMAGNET for decades. Global dipole moment decay (~5% per century, ~15 nT/yr in intensity at Earth's surface) is well-documented in geomagnetic literature (e.g., Finlay et al. 2010, IGRF-13). SAA regional decay rates of 20-35 nT/yr are tracked by INTERMAGNET and reported in WMM technical reports. The dome relabels the cause as 'aetheric field decay' instead of 'outer core convection and reversed-flux patch growth' but predicts no different numerical value.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: PROS-007 was registered 2026-03-06. The confirming data is cited as 'NOAA WMM2025 / INTERMAGNET confirms SAA region alone dropped ~30 nT since January 2025.' WMM2025 was released in late 2024, and INTERMAGNET provides continuous real-time data. Both data sources were available months before registration. The 6-day batch registration pattern (WIN-035 through WIN-039 all registered 2026-03-06) further suggests data was reviewed before predictions were formalized. The SAA region has been declining at 20-35 nT/yr for years — predicting >=28 nT decay over 12 months in a region decaying at 30+ nT/yr is trivially guaranteed.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The 28 nT/yr threshold appears in the model parameter table (raw-text/05-model.txt: 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year') but no derivation connects disc_radius (20,015 km), firmament_height (9,086 km), or any other dome geometric parameter to this specific value. The value is observationally adopted — it matches the observed SAA core decay rate from INTERMAGNET stations. No formula on the dome site starts from dome geometry and arrives at 28 nT/yr rather than any other rate. Compare with PRED-012 which projects 32 nT/yr by 2030 — also stated without derivation.

holes_found.description: Our review claims 'Global dipole moment decay is approximately 15 nT/yr per published NOAA data.' This figure needs careful scrutiny. The global dipole moment is typically expressed as a percentage decline (~5% per century) or in units of nT² (dipole moment), not simply 'nT/yr' at the surface. Surface field intensity decline varies by location — it's not a single global 'nT/yr' number. At some locations (e.g., in the SAA), decline is 20-35 nT/yr; at others (e.g., parts of Asia), the field is actually INCREASING. The global average surface field intensity decline is roughly 15-20 nT/yr, but this depends heavily on the averaging method. A dome defender could argue: 'There IS no single global nT/yr number — the dome correctly identifies >=28 nT/yr as a meaningful regional threshold, and the SAA is the most scientifically significant region to monitor.' Our review should be more precise about what '~15 nT/yr globally' means and cite a specific source.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace '~15 nT/yr globally' with a more defensible statement. Options: (1) 'The global dipole moment is declining at ~5% per century (Finlay et al. 2010), which translates to roughly 15-20 nT/yr in average surface intensity, but this varies enormously by location.' (2) Alternatively, shift the argument away from the specific rate and toward the conflation: 'PROS-007 claims to predict "global geomagnetic field" decay, but confirms it with SAA-only data. The SAA is the fastest-declining region on Earth — using it to represent the global average inflates the apparent decay rate by roughly 2×.' This framing is stronger and doesn't require us to pin down an exact global nT/yr number.

holes_found.description: Our review's detail_claim says 'Magnetic field decay exceeds 28 nT/year globally.' But PROS-007's actual wording is: 'The global geomagnetic field would have decayed by at least 28 nT in the 12 months since March 2025.' There is a subtle but important difference: '28 nT in 12 months' vs '28 nT/year' — one is a cumulative threshold, the other is a rate. Our review adds 'globally' (correctly, based on PROS-007's 'global geomagnetic field' framing), but a dome defender might argue we're misrepresenting their claim as a rate rather than a threshold. Additionally, the dome's model.txt says 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year (2000-2025)' — so the dome DOES frame it as a rate elsewhere, making our characterization defensible. But precision matters for credibility.
holes_found.recommendation: Adjust detail_claim to match PROS-007's exact framing: 'The global geomagnetic field would decay by at least 28 nT in 12 months since March 2025.' This is both more accurate and actually strengthens our rebuttal, because the confirmation explicitly uses SAA-only data for what was framed as a 'global' prediction.

holes_found.description: Our review does not note that WIN-037 and WIN-042 appear to be overlapping claims. WIN-042 in the tracker is 'Field decay >=28 nT/year' — essentially the same threshold. The dome site lists WIN-037 as 'Field Decay >=28 nT Since March 2025' (a specific time window) and WIN-042 as the ongoing rate claim. If these are substantively the same prediction applied to different time periods, this is another instance of WIN count inflation (similar to WIN-007/WIN-036 overlap identified in WIN-036 review).
holes_found.recommendation: When WIN-042 is reviewed, cross-reference with WIN-037 and assess whether they are genuinely independent predictions or the same threshold restated. Consider adding a detail_group linking WIN-037 and WIN-042. For now, add a note to WIN-037's detail_extra: 'See also WIN-042, which applies the same >=28 nT/yr threshold to a different time window — potentially inflating the WIN count for a single ongoing observation.'

holes_found.description: The 6-day registration-to-confirmation gap is not mentioned anywhere in our WIN-037 review. PROS-007 was registered 2026-03-06 and confirmed by 2026-03-12 (the same batch pattern as WIN-035 and WIN-036). This is one of the most effective arguments against the 'prospective' framing and is currently absent from our detail text.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence: 'PROS-007 was registered 6 March 2026 and confirmed within days using data already available from NOAA WMM2025 (released late 2024) and INTERMAGNET (continuously available). The 6-day turnaround, combined with the same batch registration date as WIN-035 through WIN-039, indicates the confirming data was already in hand at registration time.'

holes_found.description: The NOAA link in our detail_evidence points to https://www.noaa.gov/ (NOAA homepage), not to the specific WMM data or INTERMAGNET data that would support our '~15 nT/yr' claim. A more specific citation would strengthen credibility and allow readers to verify.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the generic NOAA link with either https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/world-magnetic-model (WMM product page) or a specific IGRF/CHAOS-7 reference for the global dipole decay rate.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's own confirmation text is the strongest evidence of conflation. PROS-007 says 'global geomagnetic field would have decayed by at least 28 nT.' The confirmation says 'SAA region alone dropped ~30 nT.' The word 'alone' is a tacit admission that the global field may not have met the threshold — the SAA regional measurement was substituted because it passed. This bait-and-switch is the core of the 'Misleading' verdict and should be quoted directly in our review.
sources: raw-text/02-wins.txt line 44: 'The global geomagnetic field would have decayed by at least 28 nT'
raw-text/02-wins.txt line 45: 'SAA region alone dropped ~30 nT since January 2025'

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's own monitoring code (monitor.py Domain 8) only checks a single INTERMAGNET station (Tsumeb, TTB) in the SAA core — it does not compute or check any global average. This means the dome's entire 'global field decay' claim is architecturally incapable of being tested globally by its own automation. The code infrastructure confirms the conflation: the dome claims 'global' but monitors 'local SAA.'
sources: WIN-004 curmudgeon review: Domain 8 monitors TTB station against >=28 nT/yr
WIN-005 curmudgeon review: 'does NOT separately track or compute' differentiated field measurements
WIN-035 curmudgeon review: Domain 8 'does NOT track African cell minimum intensity' — monitors generic SAA decay

stronger_arguments.description: The prediction has near-zero information content. The SAA core has been declining at 25-35 nT/yr for over two decades. Predicting >=28 nT decline over 12 months in a region declining at ~30 nT/yr is like predicting it will rain in Seattle in winter. The threshold was set well within the established trend, guaranteeing confirmation. A scientifically meaningful prediction would specify the rate of change of the decay rate (acceleration or deceleration), or predict when the SAA will stabilize — not whether it will continue doing what it's been doing for 25 years.
sources: CHAOS-7 paleomagnetic model: SAA core intensity 2000-2025
WMM2025 technical reports: SAA decay characterization

stronger_arguments.description: The dome lists 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year' as a core model PARAMETER (raw-text/05-model.txt), not as a derived prediction. Parameters are inputs, not outputs. If the decay rate is a fixed model parameter adopted from observation, then measuring it and finding it matches is circular — the model was configured to match the observation. This is the same structural issue as many other WINs: the 'prediction' is just the model reading back its own input.
sources: raw-text/05-model.txt line 75: 'Field Decay Rate: >=28 nT/year (2000-2025), projected 2030: -32 nT/year'

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies that the geomagnetic field is weakening and that the South Atlantic Anomaly is the most dramatic manifestation of this decline. The SAA is indeed a region of intense scientific interest — it affects satellite operations, radiation exposure on polar flights, and is one of the most significant features of the current geomagnetic epoch. Highlighting that the SAA has dropped ~30 nT in a single year is factually accurate for that region, and monitoring ongoing field decay is scientifically sound practice.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Recognizing a well-documented phenomenon is not predicting it. The geomagnetic field decay has been tracked by NOAA, IAGA (IGRF series), and ESA/Swarm for decades. WMM2025 and CHAOS-7 both characterize the SAA decay trajectory with quantified uncertainty bounds. The dome model adds no unique prediction — no mechanism for why the rate is ~28-30 nT/yr (rather than any other value), no prediction of when it will reverse, and no derivation from dome geometry. Critically, PROS-007 claimed to predict 'global' field decay but confirmed it with 'SAA region alone' data — moving the goalposts from global to regional. A genuine test would have specified which stations to monitor and what the global-average decline would be, not set a threshold guaranteed to be met by the fastest-declining region on Earth.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-037 combines several of the dome model's most characteristic methodological problems into a single WIN:

**1. Regional-to-global conflation (the bait-and-switch)**
PROS-007 explicitly predicts 'the global geomagnetic field would have decayed by at least 28 nT.' The confirmation explicitly says 'SAA region alone dropped ~30 nT.' These are not the same measurement. The SAA core is the fastest-declining region on Earth — using it to represent the global average is like measuring temperature in Death Valley and claiming global warming exceeds 50°C. Our review correctly identifies this conflation as the core problem, but the argument would be more devastating if we quoted the dome's own words side by side.

**2. Parameter masquerading as prediction**
The dome lists 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year' as a core model parameter in its architecture page. This means the value was adopted from observation (the SAA has been declining at this rate for years) and built into the model. 'Predicting' that the field will continue decaying at the rate you built into your model is circular — it's the model reading back its own input parameter.

**3. Trivial threshold with guaranteed confirmation**
The SAA core has been declining at 25-35 nT/yr for over two decades. Setting a threshold of >=28 nT over 12 months gives the prediction a failure probability near zero. This is not a discriminating test — any model that reads SAA trend data (including WMM2025) would 'predict' the same thing. A meaningful prediction would specify the second derivative (is the decay accelerating?) or predict a reversal date.

**4. Batch registration post-hoc pattern**
PROS-007 shares the 2026-03-06 registration date with WIN-035, WIN-036, WIN-038, and WIN-039. All were confirmed within days. All use data sources (WMM2025, INTERMAGNET, HeartMath) that were publicly available months before registration. The batch pattern indicates systematic data mining, not prospective prediction.

**5. Potential duplicate with WIN-042**
WIN-042 is listed as 'Field decay >=28 nT/year' — apparently the same threshold applied to a different or overlapping time window. This should be investigated when WIN-042 is reviewed.

**Our review's weakness: the '~15 nT/yr globally' claim**
This is our most vulnerable point. Surface field intensity decline varies enormously by location — there is no single universally agreed 'global nT/yr' number. The global dipole moment decline (~5% per century) translates to roughly 15-20 nT/yr in average surface intensity, but this depends on the spatial averaging method. A sophisticated dome defender could challenge: 'Show me the paper that says exactly 15 nT/yr.' We should either cite a specific reference or reframe the argument around the conflation itself (quoting the dome's own 'global' vs 'SAA alone' wording), which doesn't require us to specify the exact global rate.

**Verdict assessment**: 'Misleading' is correct and well-supported. The combination of regional-global conflation, circular parameter-as-prediction, trivial threshold, and post-hoc timing all support this verdict. Confidence 0.88 — slightly lower than recent reviews because our review text needs significant strengthening (the detail_evidence is thin and the ~15 nT/yr claim is imprecisely sourced). The verdict itself is solid; the supporting text needs work.

citations_verified: NOAA link (https://www.noaa.gov/) — resolves correctly to NOAA homepage. However, this is generic — does not point to WMM data or any specific field decay measurement.
citations_unchecked: '~15 nT/yr globally' — no specific citation provided in our review text. This claim needs a reference: Finlay et al. (2010) 'International Geomagnetic Reference Field' is one possibility, or NCEI WMM technical reports. The exact value depends on spatial averaging method and should be stated with appropriate caveats.
'20–35 nT/yr in its core' — no citation provided. Plausible range based on INTERMAGNET TTB/HER station data, but should cite WMM2025 or CHAOS-7 model outputs for SAA regional decay rates.
The dome's own PROS-007 and WIN-037 texts reference 'NOAA WMM2025 / INTERMAGNET' without specific URLs or DOIs — our review should do better than the dome's own citation standards.
